title: Pass-the-Hash Lateral Movement
briefing: Host $TARGET recorded a SUCCESSFUL network logon (Event ID 4624, logon type 3) for $USER1 using the NTLM authentication package, coming from internal host $SRC1 — with keyLength 0 and no matching interactive logon on $SRC1. In an environment that is otherwise Kerberos, an NTLM network logon to a server is the classic Pass-the-Hash tell: the attacker replayed a stolen password hash without ever knowing the plaintext. Confirm it and scope the movement.
dashboard_hint: Discover -> rule.id: 100135 (Pass-the-Hash) or 100134 (4624 type 3). Filter win.eventdata.authenticationPackageName: NTLM and win.eventdata.logonType: 3. The tell vs normal lateral movement is NTLM (not Kerberos) + keyLength 0. Correlate targetUserName = $USER1, ipAddress = $SRC1.

options: Pass-the-Hash — reused NTLM hash for lateral logon
questions.answer: Pass-the-Hash — reused NTLM hash for lateral logon
questions.explain: A successful NTLM type-3 logon (keyLength 0) from an internal host, in a Kerberos environment, is Pass-the-Hash: a stolen hash replayed without the plaintext.
All of the activity lands on $TARGET; the other hosts are unaffected.

questions.prompt: Which field is the strongest Pass-the-Hash indicator here?
options: authenticationPackageName = NTLM on a type-3 logon
questions.answer: authenticationPackageName = NTLM on a type-3 logon
questions.explain: NTLM network logon to a server (where Kerberos is expected), keyLength 0, marks hash reuse rather than a real password auth.

questions.prompt: Which internal host did the logon originate from?
questions.explain: win.eventdata.ipAddress = $SRC1 — the pivot host the attacker is operating from.

options: T1550.002 Use Alternate Authentication Material: Pass the Hash
answer: T1550.002 Use Alternate Authentication Material: Pass the Hash
questions.explain: PtH (T1550.002) over a network logon commonly maps to admin-share lateral movement (T1021.002). No brute force or Kerberoast occurred.

options: Reset $USER1's password twice to clear cached NTLM hashes
Hunt other hosts for NTLM type-3 logons by $USER1
Enforce SMB signing and restrict NTLM
Nothing — a successful logon is always benign
answer: Reset $USER1's password twice to clear cached NTLM hashes
Hunt other hosts for NTLM type-3 logons by $USER1
Enforce SMB signing and restrict NTLM
questions.explain: Treat the hash as compromised: isolate both ends, double-reset to invalidate the stolen hash, sweep for reuse elsewhere, and harden NTLM.
